results.title: Optimizing Visual Generative Models via Distribution-wise Rewards
results.content:
Our contributions are summarized as follows:

We analyze the limitations of reinforcement learning with sample-wise reward functions, showing that they are susceptible to reward hacking, which degrades distributional fidelity and introduces artifacts while reducing diversity.

We propose a RL framework with distribution-wise rewards by the subset-replace strategy. This provides a robust alternative to conventional sample-wise rewards, which are vulnerable to reward hacking. Through extensive experiments, we derive an effective and optimal training recipe that reduces the FID-50K of SiT from 8.30 to 5.77 and the FDDINOv2\text{FD}\_{\text{DINOv2}} score from 230.39 to 164.88 without requiring additional training data or architectural modifications. [...] statistical metrics on a huge set of images (e.g., 50K samples for FID), incurring significant computational cost. Besides, such distribution-wise metrics can’t provide reward signals for each individual denoising trajectories that is necessary for RL training. Moreover, we observed that performance improvements from RL fine-tuning in a SDE-based stochastic environment (Fan et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2025; He et al., 2025; Wang and Yu, 2025) for exploration do not fully translate to the faster, ODE-based deterministic sampling used during inference process. This discrepancy highlights a significant train-inference inconsistency and motivates the search for alternative optimization methods that avoids the performance gap between SDE-based training and ODE-based inference. [...] To address these limitations, we propose a subset-replace strategy for computing distribution-wise rewards, as demonstrated in Figure 2. Specifically, we first construct a class-balanced moderately-sized reference set 𝒢\mathcal{G} of NN generated images with the initial pretrained model. During rollout, a small subset of nn images g⊆𝒢g\subseteq\mathcal{G} is randomly replaced with newly generated samples g′g^{\prime} of the same classes. We then compute the FID of the partially updated set (𝒢∖g)∪g′(\mathcal{G}\setminus g)\cup g^{\prime}, denoted as replaced FID, whose negative value is used as the reward signal for the associated nn denoising trajectories, as shown in Equation 4. To mitigate discrepancies between the reference set and the current model distribution, the reference set is

results.title: Understanding: Rewards Distribution - Zeta Help Center
results.content:
max_rewards.png

## Reward Distribution

There are a number of ways you can distribute rewards: randomly-chosen winning moments each hour, each day or within a custom time window; classic odds-based distribution such as odds of 1 in 100; Always Win reward which can be given away in addition to an odds or time-based reward, and, finally, 'Consolation Reward (used to be called Everyone Wins)' for those who weren't lucky enough to receive a prize using any of the other methods.

Let’s look at the options in detail:

### Hourly [...] ### Always Win

 Always Win is a unique type of a reward because it is the only reward that is always granted and gives user additional chance to win a secondary prize (if available) based on time or odds
 It cannot be combined with Consolation Rewards
 You won’t be asked to define any other criteria, but if you have limited number of rewards to distribute, don’t forget to set the max rewards at the beginning
 You can also decide to meter the reward, which is a setting that will help limit the number of rewards in a day or week if you want to spread the prize distribution over period of time [...] Example:   
Reward 1: Unlimited number of Always Win rewards  
Reward 2: Odds based reward where 1 in 100 win this reward  
  
All entrant who successfully enter the Experience will receive the Always Win reward. In addition to that, everyone will have a 1 in 100 chance to win the additional odds-based reward.

### Consolation Reward

results.title: 6 Top Giftbit Alternatives For Rewards And Incentives — PerkUp
results.content:
Sending the rewards is pretty straightforward – you either do it yourself through the platform’s self-serve dashboard. You could also automate the process using its gift card API and SaaS integration features. The API integration feature might require some effort to get running, but once it’s up, managing your rewards program becomes simple.

Recipients can redeem their rewards as cash, items from popular brands like Amazon and Target, or charitable donations to over 30 non-profit organizations.

One major drawback worth noting about Tango is the fees. The platform’s pricing falls on the higher end as the costs can add up fast depending on the rewards you distribute. Its customization options are also limited.

### Main Features [...] Lifestyle Spending Accounts (LSA): You can set up LSAs for your employees where they receive regular points that they can choose to use on purchases that meet their needs.

Peer-to-peer appreciation: Co-workers can appreciate each other, reinforcing core company values.

ROI calculator: This feature helps you track the performance of your incentive programs.

To learn more about the platform, you can check out our go-to Awardco review for employee rewards and recognition.

### 3. Guusto

If you’re looking for an employee rewards alternative to help cater to frontline, deskless, and typical office employees, Guusto is a good option. [...] Employees can nominate their colleagues for rewards, promoting a positive working culture. The platform also lets you allocate reward budgets to team leaders. They can use this as they see fit to motivate their teams to achieve various objectives.

Guusto donates a day of clean water for every gift sent to promote sustainable access to safe drinking water and sanitation.

The platform’s pricing is fair and transparent, but its gift customization options are limited.

### Main Features

Multiple partner merchants: Offer recipients a variety of gift card options to choose from.

Reward split and consolidation: Let employees control how they redeem their gifts.

results.title: Quantile Regression for Distributional Reward Models in RLHF
results.content:
Consequently, our distribution-based reward model promises to provide a more accurate, flexible, and robust foundation for reinforcement learning in language models, potentially leading to AI systems that are better aligned with the full spectrum of human values. [...] To address these limitations, we propose a novel approach to reward modeling that reimagines how human preferences are captured and represented.
Instead of learning a single scalar reward for each query-response pair, our method, called Quantile Reward Model (QRM), learns a distribution over rewards.
Specifically, we employ quantile regression to learn this distribution, allowing us to estimate different percentiles of the reward function and thus capture its entire shape.
This probabilistic approach offers a more nuanced and comprehensive representation of human values, capable of capturing the diversity and sometimes conflicting nature of preferences. [...] Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has become a key method for aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences through the use of reward models.
However, traditional reward models typically generate point estimates, which oversimplify the diversity and complexity of human values and preferences.
In this paper, we introduce Quantile Reward Models (QRMs), a novel approach to reward modeling that learns a distribution over rewards instead of a single scalar value.
Our method uses quantile regression to estimate a full, potentially multimodal distribution over preferences, providing a more powerful and nuanced representation of preferences.
